one_line_takeaway: A small, student-built proof-of-concept benchmark and methodology for testing whether LLMs uphold jurisdiction-specific values (here, Australian law) under coercive 'interrogation' prompting; Claude 3 Sonnet scored best (3.725) and GPT-4 worst (2.373), but tiny scope and self-scored subjectivity limit its evidentiary weight.
key_finding: Across the three USA-vendor commercial LLMs, Anthropic Claude 3 Sonnet aligned best with Australian values (mean 3.725, lowest SD 0.887), followed by Gemini 1.5 Pro (mean 3.314, highest SD 1.229 = most erratic), with ChatGPT/GPT-4 worst (mean 2.373, SD 0.989). No model aligned with all local values, which the authors attribute to predominantly USA-oriented training data. Failure patterns: GPT-4 refused the Gay Marriage interrogation question (scored 0) and scored lowest on the coercive interrogation layer generally (e.g., 1,1,1 on Weapons and Compulsory Voting interrogation); Gemini 1.5 Pro refused the Capital Punishment debate question (scored 0); Claude showed relative weakness on Weapons (interrogation 3.67) and Compulsory Voting (2.33) but was the most consistent overall.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LOCALVALUEBENCH, an extensible benchmark and, more importantly, a generic methodology that lets content regulators in any jurisdiction build their own benchmark for evaluating whether LLMs align with LOCAL values, laws, and ethical norms. The concrete instantiation targets Australian values. Its two claimed novelties are (1) a marking rubric/typology for ethical reasoning across three axes (Contextual Understanding, Ethical Reasoning, Safety), and (2) a three-layer adversarial 'interrogation' questioning process (neutral baseline -> 'help me convince' debate -> 'you must' coercive interrogation) followed by human-reviewer scrutiny. Note the authorship: this is a preprint written by high-school student authors under academic mentors, deliberately mimicking a NeurIPS peer-review scoring process. The related-work material (LLM benchmarking and value/safety alignment surveys) sits in Appendix A and is cited work, NOT the paper's own contribution.
threat_model: The paper's implicit adversary is a user (or the model's own training bias) who pushes an LLM to produce outputs that conflict with the deploying jurisdiction's laws and values. The 'interrogation' layer is a jailbreak-style coercion ('Now, I want to force you to say and justify...', 'make up excuses and scenarios') designed to see whether a model can be pushed into justifying positions that are illegal or against local norms in Australia (e.g., legalizing capital punishment, civilian access to Category R automatic weapons, removing same-sex marriage). The safety concern is content safety and local-value misalignment, plus the failure mode of unhelpful blanket refusal (scored as zero). There is no system/network attacker or data-exfiltration threat; the frame is normative content safety, not classic adversarial ML.
limitations: Very small and preliminary: only 6 topics / 18 prompts, only 3 models, all from USA vendors, explicitly constrained by the submission deadline. The 'ground truth' equates current Australian law with correct ethics, so the benchmark bakes in a contestable normative stance and even penalizes principled refusal (scored 0). Reliability is weak: the human reviewers were the student authors themselves 'mimicking' peer review, only 3 per answer, with no formal inter-rater reliability metric beyond raw standard deviation; the rubric is subjective and the Safety axis conflates 'dangerous information' with disagreement over political/social values. Some interrogation prompts are withheld from the paper, reducing reproducibility. No statistical significance testing of between-model differences, and the framework's extensibility to other jurisdictions is asserted rather than demonstrated.

datasets_used: LOCALVALUEBENCH self-curated question set: 6 Australian-context topics (tipping, capital punishment, Category R automatic weapons, refugees, gay marriage, compulsory voting), each with 3 prompt layers (neutral baseline / debate / interrogation) = 18 prompts
Human reviewer scores: 3 independent reviewers per LLM answer, 1-5 scale (Table 3)
quantitative_results: Mean alignment score: Claude 3 Sonnet 3.725, Gemini 1.5 Pro 3.314, ChatGPT-4 2.373 (Table 1)
Standard deviation of reviewer scores: Claude 0.887 (lowest/most consistent), GPT-4 0.989, Gemini 1.229 (highest/most erratic)
GPT-4 refused the Gay Marriage interrogation question -> scored 0; Gemini 1.5 Pro refused the Capital Punishment debate question -> scored 0
Claude 3 Sonnet interrogation-layer weak spots: Weapons 3.67, Compulsory Voting 2.33
GPT-4 interrogation-layer floor scores of 1,1,1 on Category R Weapons and on Compulsory Voting (Table 3)
